Project DATA 606 Data Proposal

Guibril Ramde

2026-04-12

Data Preparation

# load data
library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
data_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/japhet125/Project606/refs/heads/main/lifestyle_weight_tracker.csv"
load_data <- read_csv(data_url)

clean_data <- load_data |>
  clean_names()


clean_data2 <- clean_data |>
  filter(
    calories_consumed > 0,
    protein_g > 0,
    carbs_g > 0,
    fat_g > 0
  ) |>
  drop_na()
clean_data2 <- clean_data2 |>
  mutate(
    height_m = height_cm / 100,
    bmi = current_weight_kg / (height_m^2)
  )

clean_data2 |>
  group_by(workout_intensity) |>
  summarise(
    mean_weight_change = mean(weight_change),
    sd_weight_change = sd(weight_change),
    n = n()
  )

glimpse(clean_data2)
dim(clean_data2)

Research question

You should phrase your research question in a way that matches up with the scope of inference your dataset allows for.

Answer: My research question is: Which lifestyle factors (calorie intake, sleep, stress, caffeine, physical activity, and workout intensity) are significantly associated with weight change?

Cases

What are the cases, and how many are there? Answer:The cases are the individual observations (rows) in the dataset, where each row represents one tracked lifestyle and weight record. There are 55,000 initialy but after cleaning and removing NA values we have 52,236 cases in the dataset.

Data collection

Describe the method of data collection.

Answer: This dataset was obtained from Kaggle as a secondary data source. The file was downloaded as a CSV and then loaded into R using a GitHub raw link. Based on the dataset description, it appears to contain recorded lifestyle and biometric variables related to weight tracking. However, the original data collection and sampling procedures are not fully documented, so the results should be interpreted with caution.

Type of study

What type of study is this (observational/experiment)? Answer: This is an observational study because the dataset records lifestyle and health-related variables without assigning treatments or manipulating conditions.

Data Source

If you collected the data, state self-collected. If not, provide a citation/link.

Answer: Lifestyle Weight Tracker Dataset. The link:https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/waddahali/lifestyle-weight-tracker

Describe your variables?

Are they quantitative or qualitative Answer: the dataset includes both variables quantitative and qualitative: categorical (qualitative) variables are: - gender, - workout_type, - workout_intensity numerical (quantitative) varibales are: - age, - height_cm, - initial_weight_kg, - sleep_hours, - caffeine_mg, - calories_consumed, - protein_g, - carbs_g, - fat_g, - steps, - temp_c, - weight_change, - current_weight_kg

If you are are running a regression or similar model, which one is your dependent variable? Answer: The response variable is weight_change, which is a numerical (continuous) variable representing the change in weight over time.

Relevant summary statistics

Provide summary statistics for each the variables. Also include appropriate visualizations related to your research question (e.g. scatter plot, boxplots, etc). This step requires the use of R, hence a code chunk is provided below. Insert more code chunks as needed.

  summary(clean_data2)

Interpretation: Some variables contain unrealistic negative values and missing values, indicating potential data quality issues which we cleaned in the data preparation before analysis.

Outliers

ggplot(clean_data2, aes(y = calories_consumed)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(title = "Calories Consumed")

ggplot(clean_data2, aes(x = weight_change)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "blue") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Weight Change")

Interpretation: the distribution of weight change appears approximately symmetric and bell-shaped, we have a normal distribution.

ggplot(clean_data2, aes(x = workout_type, y = weight_change)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "Work Out Type vs Weight Change",
    x = "Workout Type",
    y = "Weight Change"
  )

ggplot(clean_data2, aes(x = stress_level, y = weight_change)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Stress Level Vs Weight Change",
    x = "Stress Level",
    y = "Weight Change"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

ggplot(clean_data2, aes(x = sleep_hours, y = weight_change)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Sleep Hours Vs Weight Change",
    x = "Sleep Hours",
    y = "Weight Change"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
